last_item: Verify the read-only role is enforced at the DB level
last_result: done — added TestReadOnlyRole_DBLevelWriteDenial (integration-tagged): with the read-only role granted SELECT only, a write via the real ExecuteRead path is refused by the read-only-transaction default, and with that GUC flipped off on a real read-pool connection every operator-data write (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE/DROP) is STILL refused with 42501 insufficient_privilege — proving privilege denial, not the resettable GUC, is the authoritative boundary. Proven green against a throwaway PG14 cluster and verified non-vacuous. Reviewer findings (non-diagnostic ExecuteRead loop; cover every write variant) addressed in the restructure. Test surfaced a real residual filed as a new band-1 item.
notes: Read-only DB-level write denial proven end-to-end for operator data. Residual filed: provisionSQL's REVOKE CREATE ON SCHEMA public FROM the role does NOT strip CREATE inherited via PUBLIC on PG<=14, so the role can still create+write its own scratch tables in public when the GUC is off — next band-1 item. Remaining band-1: that CREATE-via-PUBLIC fix; secrets-at-rest (DB file 0600 + log-scrubbing test); login brute-force lockout (verify the throttle is end-to-end tested via the login handler). Integration tests need a real PG and don't run in the normal gate; go/psql are snap-sandboxed (run with sandbox disabled).
north_star: An indie hacker can stand up and run a rock-solid Postgres entirely from the indiepg UI, on best defaults, with safe optional overrides.
